title: Resource Management: Food, Water and Energy Overview
examTip: The overview lesson sets up the whole resource management topic. Make sure you know one specific UK issue for each of food, water and energy — these appear in Paper 2 Section C introductory questions.
introTips: Food, water and energy are covered together in the overview — know one UK example for each.
Resource insecurity = not having reliable access to enough of a resource. Know the causes for each.
The overview questions on Paper 2 are typically worth 4–6 marks — concise, factual answers with evidence work best.
intro: Without food you die in weeks. Without water in days. Without energy, modern economies collapse within hours. These three resources underpin every aspect of human life and development — yet access to them is deeply unequal, growing demand is putting global supplies under pressure, and climate change is making the situation more unpredictable. Understanding why resources are unevenly distributed and what that means for different countries is the starting point for one of the most important topics in AQA Geography.

keyPoints.heading: Global resource demand: why it is rising
keyPoints.content: Demand for food, water and energy is rising globally for two key reasons: population growth and economic development. The world's population grew from 2.5 billion in 1950 to 8 billion in 2022, and is projected to reach approximately 10 billion by 2050 — simply more people means more demand for all resources. But economic development matters just as much: a person in the USA consumes roughly 100 times more energy than someone in sub-Saharan Africa. As countries develop, people eat more meat (which requires far more land and water to produce than plant foods), use more electricity, and drive more cars. Resources are also distributed unevenly around the world — some regions have abundant water and fertile land, others have almost none. This mismatch between where resources are and where people need them creates geopolitical tensions and is one of the defining challenges of the 21st century.
writeBullets: Population growth — 2.5bn (1950) to 8bn (2022); projected 10bn by 2050
Economic development — richer people consume more of every resource
USA uses ~100× more energy per person than sub-Saharan Africa
Meat consumption rises with wealth — much more land/water than plant foods
Uneven distribution — some regions abundant, others scarce
Geopolitical tensions — resource competition between countries
keyPoints.memTip: More people + richer people = exponentially more demand. Both factors multiply each other.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often only think of population growth — always add economic development as the second driver of rising demand.

keyPoints.heading: Food security — global patterns and UK issues
keyPoints.content: The UN defines food security as all people at all times having access to sufficient, safe and nutritious food. Currently around 820 million people are chronically undernourished — mostly in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. At the same time, around 2 billion people globally are overweight or obese — mostly in HICs and rapidly urbanising NEEs. This is described as the 'double burden of malnutrition'. In the UK, food security is often taken for granted, but there are real vulnerabilities: the UK imports approximately 40% of its food (from the EU, South America and Africa), making supply chains vulnerable to disruption. In 2022–23, a record 3 million people used food banks, reflecting the cost of living crisis and food price inflation. Food deserts — areas where people have poor access to fresh, healthy and affordable food — are widespread in deprived urban neighbourhoods.
writeBullets: Food security — all people at all times: access to sufficient, safe, nutritious food (UN definition)
820m chronically undernourished — mostly sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia
2bn overweight/obese — HICs and urbanising NEEs; the 'double burden of malnutrition'
UK imports 40% of food — vulnerable to supply chain disruption
3m people used UK food banks in 2022–23 — record high
Food deserts — poor access to healthy food in deprived urban areas
keyPoints.memTip: 820m starving, 2bn overweight — same planet, same century. That's the double burden.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The UK food security angle is often tested — know that 40% of UK food is imported and 3m people used food banks in 2022-23.

keyPoints.heading: Water security — global patterns and UK issues
keyPoints.content: Only about 3% of Earth's water is fresh, and of that, roughly 70% is locked in ice caps and glaciers — leaving less than 1% of Earth's total water as accessible fresh water. About 2 billion people currently lack access to safe drinking water. Water-stressed regions include the Middle East, North Africa, India, northern China, western USA and Australia. Causes of water insecurity range from low rainfall (Middle East) to population pressure (India) to climate change (more drought globally). In the UK, the south and east of England experiences water stress — demand is high relative to rainfall, and chalk aquifers are under increasing pressure. UK water infrastructure is aging, and water companies have faced major criticism for discharging raw sewage into rivers and coastal waters. Hosepipe bans have become increasingly common during dry summers.
writeBullets: Only 3% of Earth's water is fresh; 70% of that locked in ice — <1% accessible
2bn people lack safe drinking water globally
Water-stressed regions — Middle East, North Africa, India, northern China, western USA
UK water stress — SE England: high demand, low relative rainfall
Chalk aquifers under pressure — key groundwater source for SE England
Raw sewage discharges — UK water companies criticised; rivers and coastal waters affected
Hosepipe bans increasingly common during summer — sign of water stress
keyPoints.memTip: 3%, 70% frozen, <1% for us — water is rarer than it looks from a blue planet.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The UK water stress point is often overlooked — many students assume the UK has plenty of water. The SE England issue is an important specific example.

keyPoints.heading: Energy security — global patterns and UK issues
keyPoints.content: Reliable, affordable access to energy is central to development — you can't run hospitals, schools or industries without it. Fossil fuels still provide approximately 80% of global energy (coal ~27%, oil ~33%, gas ~24%), with nuclear and renewables making up the rest. Energy insecurity — the inability to access reliable, affordable energy — has multiple causes: finite fossil fuels, geopolitical instability (Middle East conflict disrupts oil supply; Russia-Ukraine war in 2022 cut gas supplies to Europe, causing energy price spikes), price volatility and the need to transition to renewables (which requires huge investment). Around 770 million people have no access to electricity (2022), mostly in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. In the UK, North Sea oil and gas production is declining, the UK is importing LNG (liquefied natural gas) from the USA and Qatar, and is rapidly expanding offshore wind (which generated about 40% of UK electricity from renewables in 2023). The UK has set a legally binding target to reach net zero by 2050.
writeBullets: Fossil fuels ~80% of global energy — coal 27%, oil 33%, gas 24%
Energy insecurity causes — finite fuels, geopolitical instability, price volatility
Russia-Ukraine war 2022 — gas supply cuts; European energy prices spiked sharply
770m people have no electricity — mostly sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia
UK North Sea — oil and gas declining; importing LNG from USA and Qatar
UK offshore wind — ~40% of electricity from renewables in 2023; growing fast
UK net zero target — legally binding commitment to reach net zero by 2050
keyPoints.memTip: Fossil fuels: 80% of world energy, but running out. Renewables: 11% and climbing fast.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The Russia-Ukraine war and European gas supply is a current, real-world example of energy insecurity — very useful for Paper 2 context questions.

tables.title: Overview of food, water and energy resource management
rows: Rising — population growth and dietary change (more meat)
Drought, conflict, poverty, soil degradation, climate change
Imports 40% of food; 3m used food banks (2022–23); food deserts in deprived areas
Rising — population growth, irrigation, industry, urbanisation
Middle East, North Africa, India, northern China, western USA
Low rainfall, pollution, overuse, poor infrastructure, population pressure
SE England water stress; chalk aquifers under pressure; sewage discharges into rivers
Rising — economic development lifts demand, especially in NEEs
Sub-Saharan Africa (770m without electricity), import-dependent countries
Finite fossil fuels, geopolitical instability, price volatility, slow transition
North Sea declining; importing LNG; expanding offshore wind; net zero target 2050

1.question: What is the UN definition of food security?
options: All people at all times have access to sufficient, safe and nutritious food
All countries produce at least 80% of their own food domestically
No person in a country goes hungry for more than three consecutive days
All countries have enough food in emergency reserves for six months
1.correctFeedback: Correct! The UN definition focuses on access — not just availability. Food security means everyone, everywhere, always has enough safe and nutritious food. Access (can people afford it?) is as important as availability (does it exist?).
1.wrongFeedback: Not quite. The UN defines food security as: all people at all times having access to sufficient, safe and nutritious food. The key word is 'access' — food can exist but still be out of reach for poor people.

3.question: How did the Russia-Ukraine war (2022) create an energy security problem for Europe?
options: Russia cut gas supplies to Europe, causing energy prices to spike sharply across the continent
Ukraine closed its ports, preventing coal shipments from reaching European power stations
Russia imposed tariffs on oil, making petrol unaffordable for European consumers
European wind farms were destroyed in fighting, reducing renewable electricity generation
3.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Russia was a major supplier of natural gas to Europe — when it reduced supplies in 2022, gas prices spiked dramatically, showing how dependent Europe was on a single geopolitical supplier. This is a key example of energy insecurity.
3.wrongFeedback: Not quite. Russia cut gas supplies to Europe in 2022, causing energy prices to spike sharply. This exposed how vulnerable Europe was to a single supplier — a classic example of geopolitical energy insecurity.

commonMistakes: Saying only 'population growth' drives resource demand — always add economic development and rising consumption per person, especially in NEEs.
Thinking the UK is fully food secure because it is a rich country — the UK imports 40% of its food and 3m people used food banks in 2022-23; food insecurity exists within HICs.
Believing all of Earth's water is accessible — only <1% is fresh and accessible (3% total fresh, but 70% of that is locked in ice). Always quote this statistic.
Forgetting UK-specific resource issues — the exam asks specifically about UK food, water and energy security; have one concrete example for each ready.
Stating that the energy transition is nearly complete — fossil fuels still provide ~80% of global energy; renewables are growing but from a small base.
examTips: Overview questions (typically 4 marks) need one developed point per resource — state the issue, give evidence, explain the cause briefly.
For food security, the 'double burden' (820m hungry AND 2bn overweight) is a striking fact that shows the problem is about distribution and access, not just total food supply.
UK water stress in SE England (not lack of rain nationally, but regional pressure) distinguishes good answers from ones that say 'the UK has plenty of rain'.
Energy security is increasingly about geopolitics — the Russia-Ukraine example shows that even rich, developed countries can be vulnerable to energy insecurity.
revisionCardBullets: USA uses ~100× more energy per person than sub-Saharan Africa
Food security (UN): all people at all times — access to sufficient, safe, nutritious food
820m chronically hungry; 2bn overweight — the 'double burden of malnutrition'
UK imports 40% of food; 3m people used food banks (2022–23)
<1% of Earth's water is fresh and accessible; 2bn lack safe drinking water
UK water stress — SE England; chalk aquifers; sewage discharges controversy
Fossil fuels — ~80% of global energy (coal 27%, oil 33%, gas 24%)
770m people globally have no electricity — mainly sub-Saharan Africa
Russia-Ukraine war 2022 — gas supply cuts; European energy prices spiked
UK: North Sea declining; importing LNG; offshore wind ~40% of renewables electricity (2023)
UK net zero target — legally binding commitment to reach net zero by 2050
